text.title: Confidence Score and LLM Validation Pipeline
text.summary: Confidence score formula, pre-phase check template, LLM validation pipeline, LLM-as-judge pattern, and CI/CD gate enforcement strategies.

rule.statement: Calculate confidence as a weighted sum of four objective factors; require artifact evidence (test output, coverage report) rather than agent self-assessment.
rule.rationale: Agents treating confidence scores as checkboxes self-report inflated values. Requiring actual measurement artifacts (not just agent assertions) produces reliable scores.

example.code:
Confidence = (Automated_Pass * 0.4) + (Coverage * 0.2) + (Risk_Mitigation * 0.2) + (Traceability * 0.2)
Factors: Automated_Pass (40%): % of automated checks passing Coverage (20%): % of requirements covered by tests Risk_Mitigation (20%): % of identified risks with mitigations Traceability (20%): % of requirements traceable to implementation

## Confidence Check: [Phase Name]
### Prerequisites Met - [ ] Previous gate passed (artifact attached) - [ ] Required artifacts exist - [ ] Dependencies available - [ ] Context understood
### Current Confidence: X%
### Proceed? - [ ] YES (>= threshold) - [ ] NO (< threshold) — Reason: [...]
example.note: Every phase transition requires an explicit confidence check. "Proceed?" is a binary decision, not a sliding scale.

section.title: LLM Validation Pipeline
section.p: Standard flow for validating LLM-generated code:

list.item: L1 check: if fail, re-prompt with exact lint/type output; cap at 3 re-prompts
L2 check: if fail, re-prompt with exact test failure output; cap at 3 re-prompts
AI review: secondary model reviews for correctness and completeness
Human spot-check: business logic, security implications

rule.statement: Check LLM output against L1 immediately after generation; do not accumulate multiple files before the first gate check.
rule.rationale: Early feedback prevents context drift. An agent that writes 10 files before seeing L1 errors must revisit all 10; an agent that sees L1 errors after each file corrects patterns immediately.

rule.statement: Use a different model from the one that generated the code for L4 correctness evaluation.
rule.rationale: Generation model has systematic blind spots for its own output — it will approve its own logic errors. A secondary evaluator with fresh context catches what the generator cannot.

example.code: LLM-as-judge prompt (L4): You are an evaluator. Input: spec.md (AC-X list), test-plan.md, implementation diff. Score 0-100 on: - Correctness: implementation matches all AC? (40%) - Completeness: all requirements addressed? (20%) - Security: obvious vulnerabilities? (20%) - Maintainability: readable, follows patterns? (20%) Output: score, factor breakdown, issues blocking L4 approval. Threshold: 80% minimum.

list.item: Hard block (L1, L2): pipeline fails, no proceed — these gates are 100% automated and objective
Soft block (L3, L4): warning, require human approval to proceed — human judgment can assess context
Advisory: log issues, allow proceed — for non-critical informational checks only

rule.statement: Define gate pass criteria in constitution.md at project start, not per-feature under deadline pressure.
rule.rationale: Negotiating thresholds under deadline pressure produces inconsistent standards and technical debt. Fixed, project-level standards prevent this.
